speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me when. This is tape number 5, interview with Miriam Greenstein, May 3rd, 1996. After your liberation--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's been a long story, hasn't it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you describe what happens--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you go to Sweden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zasha, actually, just to touch briefly, convinced the doctors that I did not have tuberculosis, that I was not dying. They kept telling her, no, I would die any minute. She insisted. She pestered until they took chest x-rays. And believe it or not, I had no TB.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she proceeded to nurse me back to health. And through her, because she was just-- she was healthy. It was just, just malnutrition with her. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was taken in by a group of Polish Catholics, all inmates in Bergen-Belsen who had been freed, all of them in a hospital. Much stronger than I, not nearly as abused as the Jews, not especially selected for death. They were there because bad luck versus total annihilation of a group of human beings.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Zasha was selected for transport to Sweden. The doctor came in with an interpreter. And when I heard, in Polish, what this was all about, I remembered my few months of English from age 10. And I quickly said, doctor, me go Sweden, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he proceeded to answer me in a barrage of English, which, of course I did not understand. He got the interpreter back. And he told her to tell me that he was so sorry, but they were not taking children to Sweden. This was for grown-ups only.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And by then I was 15. I had turned 15 October 21st, and this was April. Well, probably end of May by then, mid May. And I said, doctor, I'm not a child. And he smiled at me. He looked so sad. He said, I'm sorry, little one. We just can't take you. You're too young. I said, but doctor, I'm 15. Please. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was translated. I don't remember what his face looked like, but I do remember the expression. The mouth was a perfect O. And he just stared at me. And tears started pouring down his face. He told the translator to tell me that's impossible. I might be 10, maximum. And I said no, no, I'm 15. He came over, and he took my hand, and he said, in English, OK, you go Sweden, little one. And I got on a transport to Sweden. We went through Lubeck, Germany. Had to wait for the boat there for quite a while. Very stormy crossing. Ended up in Ramlosa, in a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] isolation camp, quarantined for six weeks to make certain we had no communicable diseases to take out of the community. I had memorized my uncle's address. This is my mother's brother, who had emigrated to America before the beginning of World War II in 1938. His address was my nightly litany. And the decision had been made, whoever was alive in the family was to write to him as a contact point so we could find each other in case we had been separated. He didn't respond. I wrote three letters. There was no answer. I was becoming very dejected, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then we were moved out of quarantine to another camp. I decided to write the last letter. I was quite unpleasant in it, very short. I said, I'm sorry you don't want to acknowledge the fact that I'm alive. I'm not asking you for anything except, please, if you've heard from anyone, do answer me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Within 10 days, there was a telegram for-- waiting for me in the administrator's office. It's the only letter my uncle received. The others had been lost. I believe there was never any postage issued for them, because we had a little bit of money from the Red Cross, and we gave it to other survivors to buy the postage. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah, black market, you know? Things happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, within a few days after the telegram arrived, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my uncle had contacted the Swedish Vice Consul in Portland, Oregon, Axel Wide and his wife, Melanie. They in turn contacted their, I believe it was his, aunt and uncle in Landskrona, Sweden. They were part of the Royal Swedish family, the Bernadotte family. Uncle Nils, as I came to know and love him and call him, was a Lutheran minister, and he was a city councilman. Tante Maria was a housewife. They were beautiful, beautiful people. They had one daughter who was 26, lived in another city. But she'd come home to visit me. We communicated in German, if you can believe. I didn't speak Swedish. They did not speak Polish. My English was nonexistent. German was fluent. I lived with them. They gave me a home. They gave up their post- World War II ration cards in Sweden. Sweden was-- on-- had a shortage of everything. To outfit me, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Uncle Nils took me first to Stockholm, I believe, to the American Consulate, to get my papers, back again, then to Oslo, Norway, where we spent about three days. He walked me aboard the ship, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] made certain I had a Swedish roommate. I had the upper bunk. She had the lower. It was probably 4 feet wide by-- the bunk-- by 6 feet, the whole-- it was third class on the SS And he stood at the wharf as I stood on top of the deck waving, as long as there was a speck. We sailed off. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] About 10 days later, we reached the shores of America. This was an interesting ship. Third class. We're all youngsters who were going to visit, to live with their families in the Americas, not just the United States. There were two sisters who went on to Costa Rica. I don't remember where the others went. All of us no more than 18. Average age was probably 15. I turned 16 just before I left Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what day did you arrive in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: November 26, 1945. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We stood in a downpour, and we watched the skyline of New York City through a haze. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we saw her. You know who she is. The lady. I still cry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We stood there and cried and cried and cried. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the next morning, debarkation started. I had a telegram from my aunt and uncle, Maurice and Henita Jakobi from Portland, Oregon. They had traveled cross-country to New York to meet me. But they were waiting for me as soon as I got through customs and inspection. I believe I was still on Ellis Island, though I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We recognized each other. Fell into their arms. They put me between them in the cab, straight to the Taft Hotel in New York City, where we spent almost two weeks. My aunt was outfitting me. It was the Americanization of Miriam. [LAUGHS] That's when I became Miriam versus [NON-ENGLISH]. I thought I was so American. I had signed my letters, Mary. My uncle said that wasn't acceptable. [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We came to Portland, Oregon, where I started a new life. I lost everybody in Europe. If I didn't mention it before, I found out in 1942 that my father had died of typhus in the Poznan labor camp, Posen in German. I found out through a couple of sisters in Bergen-Belsen that they had seen my mother in Auschwitz. They didn't know she was my mother. They referred to her as that crazy woman who was screaming her daughter's name. And they were sure she was dead by then, that they would have taken her to the gas chambers. When I told them I was her daughter, they wouldn't talk to me. There is no trace, no record of my mother anywhere. The last record I have is in the Lodz Ghetto, from the Lodz papers, where we lived. Not date of departure or deportation, because we were on the last transports. Of course, nobody was keeping records by then. And I started in American high school, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In closing, do you want to leave a message for your children and grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: My children and grandchildren know how precious freedom is, how wonderful liberty is. They were raised with those principles. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I hope, I believe, that they are not prejudiced in any way. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I hope they have a good life. I've learned many survivors' childrens, children have certain types of physical problems, more prone than others, I don't know. I pray for a good, happy life for all of them, and they know I love them. And it's a miracle that I conceived [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and that they were all born in perfect health, because the gynecologist who gave me the first exam in Portland did not think I would ever conceive a child. Proved him wrong. Four daughters.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the odds of that happening? Do you want to say something? Not going to put that on tape. I don't care. I don't need it.

words.text: Not going to put that on tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you can-- I mean, you've already spoken about, a little bit, about the opportunity to be able to have children and how important that is, and this is living proof of your whole family. So do you want to just do the, the speaking, and if anyone else wants to chime in, you can. Feel free to do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: You can. Look at her. [LAUGHTER] You're a good--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mommy.

speaker: interviewee
text: --talker.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can hum something. I can sing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Almost ready? OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Hitler did not defeat us. This is my family, my immediate family minus one daughter and granddaughter, Helena and Kelsey Fagan, who live in Juneau, Alaska. They have sent a photograph. And I think we are going to start introducing with the littlest. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is Alexis Mills. Say hi. Wave. Wave to the lady.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Alex.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you're Alex. I'm so sorry. Wave to the lady, honey.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you wave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you wave to her? She is three years old, and she's my great-granddaughter. Sitting next to her is her mommy, Kimberly Mills, who is my oldest granddaughter. Then this is Kim's sister, Julie Amato, who is Alexis', Alex's--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Alex.

speaker: interviewee
text: --auntie. Alex. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Alex.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is Nathan Horton, who belongs to Cathy and John Horton. Cathy is my youngest daughter. John is her husband, my son-in law. And on this side, we have Stephanie, who is going to be 12 in a few days. And her teacher wants me to come and talk at school to her class, but I think we've run out of time. And her mommy, my daughter Audrey, and daddy, my son-in-law Rich Dobson. These are the Dobsons. Behind me, minus one, is my oldest daughter, Ruth, her husband John Hipes, and my oldest grandson, not oldest grandchild. The only other boy we have, Steve Amato, Ruth's youngest, is missing. He is at work, so he could not make it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And all I can say is, when I watched people were sick watching the movie. I kept thinking, they were the lucky ones. They had someone to help them. I would have gladly traded places.

speaker: interviewer
text: However, the thing that impressed me most was the ending. The march of the living past his grave. Out of 1,100 survivors, there where how many? Maybe you know the number, Janice. I, I think 7,000 or something descendents? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A great number.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I do this. I do a lot of talking, especially to school children, to try and prevent another tragedy. It's happening all over the world. It is not another Holocaust, though it's been compared, but it is ethnic cleansing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I talk so that these kids can have a future. And I hope, when I can't do it anymore, that my children and grandchildren [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] will carry on the fight for equality and no prejudice and just no room for hatred in this world. My daughters never knew their grandparents. They never knew an aunt, an uncle of mine or a cousin. They knew my aunt and uncle here, Henita and Maurice Jakobi, who became their grandparents. They knew my parents and my cousins from photographs and from the stories I told them. And they really did not know that I was a survivor of the camps and what happened until they were quite a bit older, because mother's stories always [BACKGROUND NOISE] ended with childhood. They knew all about me being a dumb Polack, a happy kid. The rest came much later. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And once I started, I haven't stopped. It was never a secret, but I wanted them to have a normal childhood. My nightmares were my own. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And my husband, who's been dead for 11 years, was there for me through thick and thin, even though I never knew about it. He'd just say to me, another bad night, huh, honey? You were screaming. And I'd say, what? [LAUGHS] They were my nightmares. So thank you for this opportunity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a picture of my grandfather, my mother's dad. His name was Zigmund Jakubowski. I don't know what year that picture was taken in. It's not a very flattering photo, however. I'm fortunate to have found a few pictures among my uncle's belongings, so we're using whatever I get access to. This is my maternal grandmother, Zigmund's wife, Helena Jakubowska. In Polish, there were three genders, male, female, and neuter. That's why there is this change in last names. I adored her. She was a little woman with tremendous patience, love, and strength. Both of them were shipped off to Chelmno September 12th, 1942 from the Lodz Ghetto. This is a wedding picture of my parents. Ruta Kominkowska married Zalmen-- excuse me, Ruta Jakubowska married Zalmen Kominkowski on February 28, 1928. Ironically, they were married in the city of Lodz. I am in the center with my mother and father, probably circa 1936 or '37. I can't remember the occasion, but the big bow in my hair tells me that it was a party of some kind, possibly a holiday. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As you can see, we were a happy threesome. I remember that well. My mother's aunt was a famous photographer in Poland. This was taken in her studio in the resort city of Ciechocinek, Poland. That was my very favorite doll. She was an antique by then. Her name was Helenka, after my grandma, and she had belonged to my mother as a little girl. That was taken in 1936. I was six years old. My mother, her brother Maurice, and I, again in the resort town of Ciechocinek, Poland. He was my very favorite uncle. He and my mother were 11 months apart, like twins. He was a very debonair bachelor, and I loved it when he came to visit us. It was always so much fun. He's the one who brought me, after the war, to Portland, Oregon. Dad's motorcycle. I've been told since that he was the only person in the town with a motorcycle. It was very exciting. I went with him all over the countryside. That is my father and I. I sat on a baggage rack, and we would just travel at breakneck speed to the outlying virgin forest they had, other towns. My mother trembled. She was sure something would happen to us. And I loved it. I believe I owe that spirit of adventure a thank you. I'm probably alive because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of my independent tomboy ways. A few years ago, I started painting from memory, scenes that are very vivid in my mind's eye of the Holocaust, my experiences and where I had been. I have painted throughout most of my adult life. However, they were always happy, bright, colorful pictures. This is what and how I remember Bergen-Belsen in the winter of '45, the snow, the dead bodies, the frozen people, and a blank, empty nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: --worry about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was just having trouble getting locked in on it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am in the center. I don't even know the names nor remember a thing about the two women flanking me. This is in Sweden, post liberation. They are Polish Catholics. We had all been liberated in Bergen-Belsen together, and I traveled with them and some others on the transport to Sweden from Germany. And they were my guardian angels and protectors. They helped me to regain whatever there was to find. Help, dignity, youth. In the center are the Larsgrds, the people who gave me a home in Sweden. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As you can see, he's very dignified, yet they were warm and gentle and kind. Flanking them, the young woman in the glasses is their daughter [NON-ENGLISH]. On the other side, is their niece, [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know what happened to [NON-ENGLISH]. The three Larsgrds have all been dead for many years. They were wonderful, wonderful human beings. This is the last picture I have of my Aunt Henita and Uncle Maurice together. It was taken just a few months before she died of cancer in 1968. She was my second mother. He was my second father. They gave a poor little kid another chance in life. One of the very few pictures that I have of my beloved husband, Tole Greenstein. Unfortunately, this was taken for his speaking engagements. It's for a flyer. Not the best one, but it's the last one I have of him. He died 11 years ago of a malignant brain tumor, immediately after his 60th birthday. He's the father of all of my children. We were married for almost 37 years. My daughter Helena Fagan on a train someplace in Europe. She lives in Juneau, Alaska and could not be here. The next picture is of her daughter Kelsey, in Ireland as an exchange student. Kelsey Heather Fagan, my granddaughter. This is me Irish lass. Her daddy was Irish. He also died of cancer. She was an exchange student in Ireland, and this was a picture her overlooking the ocean in the village of Rush.
